references.title: Machine Learning Prediction of University Student Dropout: Does Preference Play a Key Role?

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Predict Dropout at the end of the First Year
extracted.Data sources: Socio-Economic Data, Enrollment Variables, School Characteristics, Pre-University Variables, Preferences, !st Semester Academic Performance
extracted.Features: [Academic Amount, Degree, Area, Family Township, Admissioin Option, Gender, Country of Birth, Admission Study, Access Specialty, Follow tge Path, Time Commitment, PAU grade, Access Grade, Mother’s Education Level, Father’s Education Level, Scholarship holder, School Location, PAU Call, Admission Reason, Age]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors perform Feature Selection on the Feature Set. Authors make an analysis that is Global, followed by Degree Specific Analysis.
extracted.Models:
NN
SVM
kNN
DT
Logistic Regression
extracted.Comments: Results published are not only Total (the ones revealed here) but are also shown in terms of program-specific. The authors refer to Class-Specific Accuracy which I’m understanding to be Recall.
extracted.Limitations:
Model-Agnostic Model does not seem to perform very well when the student population is heterogenous. 

Simple family of algorithms.

No interaction data. Moreover, the most effective models. 

Holdout method seems week for a dataser as small as this one.

Unclear Feature Selection strategy. 

Only first-year dropouts are considered. 

No model optimization efforts seem to be done.

Model-Agnostic Model does not seem to perform very well when the student population is heterogenous.

annotations.text: Dropout prediction is a very popular learning analytics activity - after all, detecting students who are less likely to complete their programs is essential to prevent churn. The work uses data from first-year students attending a European University at two different moments of prediction: the beginning of the course or the end of the first semester.

Different classifiers were used, and predictions at the end of the first semester outperformed the beginning of the course. Moreover, Logistic Regression was good enough as a baseline, but was outperformed by other ML techniques.
